author: @glencahalin4786
text:
My thoughts are based on the standard cross section of gutters and downpipes have become standard because they're the sizes that account for the volume of water needing moving during a heavy downpour. eg the house design having 1 downpipe for each Xm^2 of roof area. So restricting that cross section area, as per this design, will likely cause backup during such times and possibly overflow of the guttering, though mitigated somewhat by the then water column build up pressurising the water that in itself can cause a problem as at such times energy capture section has to be able to deal with the extra pressure of up to a few meters of water column.

Admittedly it should only be a problem during the heavy downpours but I think possibly another way to go about it, would be to design the capture device bigger, so it's restricted cross section area is roughly equal to a normal downpipe's cross section, and so avoid the possible problem. One way to go about that, when happy with your basic design,  would be to flair out the top and bottom sections ( keeping it as an easy to add inline device ) to the required size for the resized capture device.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: Another ( possibly easier ) option might be to stick with the current size and see if it does in your situation. If is does turn out to be a problem take a branch off ( say 1m, this height becomes a pressure limiting device with no moving parts to maintain or wear out, a good thing ;-)  ) above the device that goes to a second downpipe ( possibly with it's own generator in it ) and re-join the downpipe below the device(s) with a Y section.

author: @kadmow
text:
thanks Rob  - - services to physics.  NB.- as soon as time gets involved we are interested in power (unless working backwards we start with power and want to compute the energy expended or gained".  (I went off in the first few seconds, not realising you were about to "present the derivations" - well her is my go at joining the dots (pun intended,  - scalar units only) with a little dimensional  commentary.

General Derivation - Energy is WORK done (or the ability to do work) = Force *distance. (I learned a long time ago that unnecessary work is MAd  - "mas" was the formula while Power  was "Work / time"  - the time rate of energy "transferred" - used or gained (total work done is Energy "exchanged").

Power  = Work done / time = = Energy expended / time =  Force * speed (quantities) = (W=N*m/s) - units  = F L /t -(dimensions) - dimensional analysis (Mass, Length and Time (M,L,t) are fundamental physical units) lets kids work out which quantities are "equivalent")

Power (W)* time (s)  = Energy (J)

Force (N) = F = mass * acceleration =  kg * m/s^2  = M L/t^2 

Energy = force * distance (J = N*m = F L ) = M L/t^2 * L = x * M ( L^2 / t^2)

(redefine) Power = kg m/s^2 * m/s = kg m^2 / s^3 = M (L^2 /t^3)

Newton 1 started with Momentum =  = mass * velocity = mv  = kg m/s  = M L/t  

(the body in space continues in a straight line at constant velocity until  acted upon by an external force...)

Newton 2,  Force  = ma - Inertia - that "property" of mass's resistance to acceleration.
Newton 3 ,  forces act equally on al bodies - equally and oppositely  (elastic collision mechanics).

(non relativistic space is nice, but not very real)

Kinetic energy is merely the integral of momentum = = 1/2 m * v^2 = 1/2 M * (L/t)^2 =  1/2 kg (m/s)^2  = x *  M (L/t)^2 =x *  M (L^2 / t^2)


Potential energy = mgh = = m a h = kg * m/s^2 * m = M L/s^2 L = x * M ( L^2/t^2)


I think this is all right - think of it as a bit of an brain stretch for the kids (and others who may or may not have been previously  presented with the simple interrelations)

author: @dariusj33
text:
That's a nice PoC! However, I didn't quite catch what role does the 3m height play in all this. Yes, you're dumping water from 3m height, but then it stops right at the top of your generator segment and accumulates, because it has to flow through a tiny slit - not the whole Ø10cm or whatever diameter. I mean, the falling water loses speed and, consequently, energy, because it's absorbed by the top part of the generator segment.

I think you should either narrow down the diameter of the pipe to +/- match the slit (or create a slope) OR make a bigger water-wheel that's fins are +/- the diameter of the pipe. Effectively, more than half of the water wheel will be outside of the pipe, but only the effective area of it will be hit by the falling water.

I understand why you made the slit - so that water would only fall on the effective area of the water wheel and wouldn't cause resistance on the other side. But this introduces two problems:
1. thinner duct (slit) will require a lot more maintenance (leaves, branches, feathers and other junk will clog it very easily)
2. you're losing the "falling water from 3 meters" effect by stopping it like 2cm from the water wheel.

I bet the only reason your generator was spinning is that the accumulated water rose in height inside the pipe and created more pressure, so the accumulated water jetted on the fins. If I remember my classical physics lessons well, the height (depth) of the liquid (water) mass is what determines its pressure.

replies.author: @petehiggins33
replies.text: Absolutely correct.  Basically if the water touches anything at all before it hits the turbine then it has lost all its energy.  The only reason the paddle turns is that it has no load resistance.

author: @steved4429
text:
I thought of using water as a battery, pumping it up a height, when surplus (from solar) and reclaiming via gravity.
But when I ran the numbers, a ton of water raised a meter, takes (lose less) 9800 Joules, or about the same as an AA battery! tell people you could life a ton with one battery, and they are usually incredulous.
Not knocking your idea though, as you say, it's simple, cheap, and uses free energy that normally goes down the drain! 
Just did some back of envelope sums, my house has a 40M2 roof, x 25L per square M per one inch of rain (sorry about mixed units!). So a ton of water every inch, it rains 70 odd inches a year here in Wales, and my drop is about 5 meters. 3.4 million Joules! about 1KWh without losses.
Trouble is, I'd have to drive for a while to get some "English" downpipe😂

replies.author: @stephen-boddy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You're trying to extract kinetic energy which itself comes from the stored potential energy. Most of the falling water will slap the mostly flat plate and lose it's K.E. to sound, heat and vibration etc. There is then very little potential energy left to convert into K.E. as it flows down through the slot. The only way this works is if you fill the entire 3 meter pipe with a head of water and the pressure acts like a compressor to rapidly accelerate the water as it forces it's way out of the slot. Even in the UK, I don't know that we have heavy enough rain, often enough, to optimize for that. Or you need a siphon (which I think you may have mentioned) and the prospect of storing partial loads of heavy water in your gutters when the rain ends.

It would be way more effective if the slot was 3m long so all the accumulated K.E. was applied only to the turbine.

author: @PetraKann
text:
The apple on the table does not have zero energy. It’s potential energy is equal to mgH where H is the distance the apple is from the centre if the earth (or rather the centre if gravity of the earth)
You added potential energy by lifting  the apple above the table.
The total energy is equal to the potential energy + kinetic energy. The total energy is constant.
Of course there are other forms energy can take.
In fact photons have energy even though they possess no mass: this energy can be determined by knowing the frequency or wavelength of the light. (E=hν).
You’re right in saying that answering the question “what is energy?” Is not easy to answer.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a photon doesn't have a rest mass and that's only to satisfy relativity as light travels at the speed of light lol

replies.author: @PetraKann
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering A photon's mass is always zero: it's a boson. Bosons also lack charge and have one unit of spin.
Energy, as you stated in your video is a tricky concept and takes many forms (even though the Units of Joule remain the same).

E=mc2 is a good example of this. The "m" term is actually the rest mass and should be written differently - say m0.And the E term is the rest Energy - E0

The original general Einstein equations is written:

E^2 = p^2c^2 + m0^2 c^4

You can see that when an object is at rest, the momentum "p" is zero so the first term p^2c^2 is equal to zero.
So you can simplify the above equation to

E^2 = m0^2c^4

Now you can take the square root of both sides and you obtain the famous Einstein equation E=mc2.
Or rather E0 = m0c2
This equation does not apply to bosons like photons because the m0 term is zero.

The other thing to notice is that when you take the square root, you end up with two solutions: the positive and negative solution.

So E0 = +/- m0c2

What does negative m0c2 imply Mr Smith?

replies.author: @MrMartinSchou
replies.text:
Sure, but that doesn't really work how you think it will.

Having multiple devices in series means the water won't actually experience a full 3 meters of fall. It will instead experience a fall that is the distance between the devices installed. And since there are no 100% efficient devices, you will end up with less energy extracted with multiple devices in series than if you only had one.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
While you have the drop on only half of the wheel, it would benefit you a lot to not merely drop it on half the wheel, but taper the whole of the water at a bevel [roughly 73.5 degrees is seen as pretty close to optimal] and direct the impingement of the water right at the 90 degree point thus developing optimal torque, at optimal slip stream flow of the water.

Also, because it is trying to deflect the water in all directions at once, one should either print flat plates onto the ends of the deflector, or add them on after the print is done.

They need not be printed, and need not even be the same material.

They could be the ends of a soup can, some builder's board, or a pair of washers.

Just glue them on, so that the water can not slip off each end, and therefore forcing it to react with the blades of the wheel, getting more effective torque out of it.

These changes would really not add much complexity or cost, and would increase the potential yield as much as 30%, and depending on the height of the water drop, due to how water accelerates more rapidly as more vertical pressure is applied, overcoming more of the natural viscosity and surface tension drag of the water inside of the tube, more net power would come out.

While those percentages may not be seen as much, for as little additional material and effort as they would increase the project by, it should certainly be viewed as worth the small amount of additional work to put those alterations into effect.

replies.author: @DonXisco
replies.text: agree about the water bevel or making it bigger so the opening to the water wheel is closer to the diameter of the down pipe.  As is, with the small hole and flat bottom, the water hits this choke point and shoots out the side through the seem of the pipe and the device instead of flowing through the wheel.  Even if it was water tight it would clog there and lose much of the energy gained from the roof height.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@DonXisco Well...

Let us see if Robert decides to add the listed improvements to his design or not.

He does some times listen to suggestions given to him.

And yes, he loses a TON of power where his joint does not seal.

But that one, I'm pretty sure he is aware of...

A little bit of silicone, hot glue, super glue, or several other products would resolve the leak, possibly even PVC pipe cement from the hardware store.

We shall see if he addresses these issues or not. /( 'w')~

author: @Boslow123
text: I'd recently thought about this. If you use 6" or 8" downpipe you would have considerably more mass stored. The water then needs to be released through a narrow hole, to create higher pressure, and longer release time, to use with the generator. Great video as always!! Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the smaller hole won't increase the energy mate - the energy is just the energy if you create higher pressure that has to come from somewhere and the release of the higher pressure comes at the cost of lower volume - so the energy remains the same

replies.author: @Boslow123
replies.text:
Sorry, I didn't mean like that, it's not to increase the energy. It would be better with an adjustable release. It's so that as it's raining, it releases slower so that the head is maintained, and energy is extracted slowly, more like a trickle charge. 
The release could be variable, dependant on how "heavy" the rain is(a sensor in the gutter perhaps so as it fills up the release opens more)
Or is it better to just flush it all at once? I live in Wales so we get lots of rain.

replies.author: @mikejones-vd3fg
replies.text: Lester Allen Pelton also thought about this when he designed the Pelton Wheel in the 1870s  " The Pelton wheel extracts energy from the impulse of moving water, as opposed to water's dead weight like the traditional overshot water wheel."  - Pelton Wheel wikipedia, its used in todays mini hydro generators, basically the turbine blades are shaped like spoons or cups that apparently convert all the waters energy into the wheel.  A speed at the rim desing.  Theres a formula on there that calculates a turbines efficiencey and this design is suitable for low flow high preasure, im guess would  be water, where as a wind turbine might be considered a high flow low preasure situation.  Apparently its the most efficient design for water, but maybe a more efficient 3d printed design exists?

replies.author: @Boslow123
replies.text:
I was just rewatching a downpipe power video with a pelton wheel(I couldn't remember the name but could see it in my head)
I suppose the only energy you will get out is limited by the amount actually falling on the roof, which generally is not really that much in the grand scheme of things.
And occasionally.....way too much.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
@Boslow123 Well, 1 inch of water per square foot is 0.62 gallons. 
Average house roof in the US is 3,000 square feet (278.7091 sq. meters).
Thus, one inch of rain will give us 1,860 gallons of water, or 7040 Litres.
Since I live in Seattle, I think this might be worthwhile.
Sorry for doing the math in Freedom Units, "It is what I am accustomed to."

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: The really cool thing about this idea is that the higher you build the collector, the more potential energy you can actually use from the rain. The downside being cost of building such a tall structure, and for the best results a large area would be needed to harvest as much water as possible. (Assuming it rains too).

replies.author: @RandomSmith
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If you changed the design so that you captured the flow more efficiently, then increasing the height would generate more. Current design only has a narrow slit and a flat area which would probably back up in the pipe generating heat/sound and a stored mass of water. So if you still wanted to go with a completely inline rather than a loop the loop sticking out the side of the pipe or something like that, you could add a funnel above so that the water didn't get slowed down or design it so the blades matched the flow coming throught the split - so the mass of water would be forcing the water through the slit more like a water wheel or spoons. In Brisbane Australia when we have a downpour our 90mm downpipes don't contain a diameters worth of flow going into our water tank so a funnel could work quite well. (Thinking here a streamlined funnel rather than a funnel one might use for adding vinegar to a table bottle. Something more like in some of your other wind videos)
